text: Hook. Hebrew, "basket of summer fruit." Septuagint, "bird-cage or net." (Haydock)<hr>Israel was ripe for destruction, ver. 2., and Amos 7:8. (Calmet)<hr>Not only those who were near, (4 Kings 15:29.) but the rest also were taken, (4 Kings 17:6.) as we pull with a hook the fruit which we cannot reach otherwise. (Worthington)

Temple, when God comes like a mighty warrior; or when the profane temples shall be pillaged, Amos 9:1. Hebrew also, "the canticles of the temple or palace shall be changed into lamentations."<hr>Place. Hebrew, "a multitude of dead bodies shall be cast in every place. Keep silence." (Calmet)

Month: the first day was observed as a festival, Numbers 10:10. (Haydock)<hr>At the expiration of the month usurers demanded their money. (Horace, 1:sat. 3.; Aristophanes, Nub. 2:1.)<hr>Corn, to sell after the sabbatical year, when it was dearest. Sabbath also denotes all "festivals." These misers think that there are too many.<hr>Sicle. Having a large measure to buy, and a small one to sell again, Deuteronomy 25:13., and Proverbs 20:10.
Shoes, for almost nothing. Thus they forced the poor to serve, or to sell their effects.
Jacob, because the rich despise the poor. It may also mean, that he swore by heaven, or the temple, (Leviticus 26:19.) or that he would destroy the high places. (Calmet)

Altogether. Septuagint, "its total ruin shall rise as a river."<hr>Egypt. The whole land shall be visited with misery, as Egypt is by the Nile. (Haydock)<hr>The enemy shall retire with the booty. The Nile overflows in summer, and covers Egypt for six weeks, carrying much earth with its impetuous waves, Isaias 18:2.

Light. Usher (the year of the world 3213.) explains this of an eclipse, at Pentecost. The Fathers generally understand that which accompanied the death of Christ; but it only implies great desolation and terror, Jeremias 15:9., and Joel 3:11. (St. Jerome, etc.) (Calmet)<hr>In their greatest prosperity, calamities shall unexpectedly fall upon them. (Worthington)
Baldness, the hair being cut in mourning, Job 1:20.<hr>Son, most afflicted, Zacharias 12:10., and Jeremias 6:26. (Calmet)

Lord. During the siege provisions were wanting, but instruction still more so. (Worthington)<hr>Israel had banished Amos. They would be left destitute. We find no prophet among them during the captivity, except Tobias, Tobias 13:3. We may apply this to the state of the Jews since the death of Christ. They have no guides. (Calmet)<hr>They read incessantly, and do not understand (St. Jerome; Mercer.) the Bible, which none will ever penetrate who refuse to receive the key from the Church. (Haydock)
Sea to sea: from west to south, or to the ocean; in whatever part of the world they may be. (Calmet)

Sin. Septuagint, "propitiation," which the pagans deemed requisite; (Horace, 1:ode 2.) or worship (Haydock) of Baal, (4 Kings 17:16.; Calmet) and all the other superstitions. (Haydock)<hr>Way. Septuagint, "thy God," or religion, (Acts 9:2.) or pilgrimage to Bersabee, Amos 5:5. Perhaps the true God was here adored; but it was in a manner which he condemned. (Calmet)<hr>In vain do those pretend to honour Him, who follow the traditions of unbelieving men. (Haydock)
